frontmatter.title: Chapter 6 — The Problem with Raw Pixels

children.value: Why Direct Pixel Comparison Fails — and Where Normalisation Hits Its Ceiling

children.label: Why Direct Pixel Comparison Fails — and Where Normalisation Hits Its Ceiling

children.value: Part I built a complete picture of everything that corrupts a pixel value. Part II asks: given that corruption, can we still compare images reliably? The answer is: sometimes yes, sometimes no — and the boundary between those two cases is the most important concept in classical computer vision.
Pixel comparison means: given a reference patch

SSD fails on all three. The question is: which failures are fixable by math, and which are not?

children.value: perfectly. But the real world produces transforms that move

children.html: bbb
aaa

children.value: Normalisation operates on pixel values at fixed positions. Once the grid shifts — due to rotation, scale, or viewpoint — there is no per-pixel math that can compensate. You need something that is invariant to spatial transforms, not just intensity transforms.
The normalisation ceiling is where classical template matching ends and feature-based methods begin.
(SIFT, HOG): describe local structure in a way that is explicitly invariant to rotation and scale — by design.
s): learn to extract representations that are invariant to whatever transforms appear in the training data — by optimisation.
Both approaches abandon the pixel value as the fundamental unit of comparison. Instead they compute a
— a vector of derived quantities that encodes
This transition — from pixels to features — is the subject of Parts IV and V.

children.alt: Interpolation under rotation — pixel values change even when scene content is the same

Rotation, scale, viewpoint cannot be fixed by per-pixel math
: Part IV provides the mathematical foundations that underpin the noise model (Chapter 2) and the normalisation theory (Chapter 6) at a deeper level.
